data.title: Millionaires Say 'Tax Us. Tax The Super Rich' In Blunt Letter Demanding Action — 'Extreme Wealth Has Cost Everyone Else Everything Else'
data.description:
More than 250 millionaires and billionaires are demanding what many in their ranks aim to avoid: higher taxes on themselves.

In a letter reported by Fortune and released during the 2026 World Economic Forum in Davos, the signers make their message unmistakable. "Tax us. Tax the super rich," they write. "Extreme wealth has cost everyone else everything else."

Power, Not Just Wealth

The letter accuses the richest 1% of concentrating not only money but control. "A handful of global oligarchs with extreme wealth have bought up our democracies; taken over our governments; gagged the freedom of our media; placed a stranglehold on technology and innovation; deepened poverty and social exclusion; and accelerated the breakdown of our planet," it reads. 

Don't Miss:

  • Fast Company Calls It a ‘Groundbreaking Step for the Creator Economy' — Investors Can Still Get In at $0.85/Share
  • Warren Buffett once said, "If you don't find a way to make money while you sleep, you will work until you die." Here’s how you can earn passive income with just $10, starting today.

According to the authors, the world is more connected than ever, yet dangerously unequal—and the damage isn't limited to numbers. "What we treasure, rich and poor alike, is being eaten away by those intent on growing the gulf between their vast power and everyone else," they wrote. 

No More Subtlety

The letter doesn't ask. It demands. "Stop squandering the time we have—tax the super rich." And it calls out the people elected to lead. "You already have a simple and effective solution, supported by millionaires and the public alike. It's your duty to deliver it."

The authors say the cost of inaction is democratic erosion, global instability, and a future controlled by a shrinking group of power players. "We want our democracies back. We want our communities back. We want our future back."

Trending: Americans With a Financial Plan Can 4X Their Wealth — Get Your Personalized Plan from a CFP Pro

A Growing Divide Among the Elite

But while some of the world's wealthiest are calling for reform, others are threatening to flee. "This is not a tax — this is asset seizure," billionaire venture capitalist and White House AI and crypto czar David Sacks told CNBC, after relocating to Texas following 30 years in California.

Hollywood attorney Alex Spiro, whose clients include entertainment and tech billionaires, warned Gov. Gavin Newsom in a formal letter that the tax would drive the ultra-rich out of the state.

Opposition isn't limited to the U.S. In France, LVMH CEO Bernard Arnault has condemned wealth taxes as "deadly."

A Line Drawn in 2026

The millionaires behind the Davos letter frame this moment as pivotal. "In 2026, we must do more than hope for a future we want," they wrote. "We must win it."

And for those unsure whether this is just symbolism, they close with a commitment: "Millionaires like us refuse to be silent. It is time to be counted."

The Call Isn't New—But It's Louder Now

That debate over whether the ultra‑wealthy should pay more isn't new. In 2011, Warren Buffett wrote in a widely discussed New York Times op‑ed titled "Stop Coddling the Super‑Rich" that it was wrong that people like him could pay a lower effective tax rate than their secretaries, and he urged lawmakers to raise taxes on the richest Americans to help address deficits and inequities in the tax code. 

Bill Gates has also voiced support for higher tax rates on the wealthy on multiple occasions. In a 2018 interview with CNN, he said, "I need to pay higher taxes" and acknowledged he had paid more than any other taxpayer over time but suggested that the government should require people in his position to pay significantly more.

He reiterated the message in a 2019 year-end blog post on GatesNotes, writing: "That's why I'm for a tax system in which, if you have more money, you pay a higher percentage in taxes. And I think the rich should pay more than they currently do, and that includes Melinda and me."

The question now is whether governments will listen to the group asking to be taxed—or the group threatening to leave.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Steel Dynamics Smashes 52-Week High On Record Steel Shipments
data.description:
Steel Dynamics, Inc. (NASDAQ:STLD) stock rose on Monday after the company reported fourth-quarter and full-year 2025 financial results.

The company reported fourth-quarter net sales of $4.414 billion. Net income attributable to Steel Dynamics totaled $266 million in the fourth quarter, or $1.82 per diluted share, compared with $207 million, or $1.36 per diluted share, in the prior-year period.

Revenue of $4.414 billion missed an analyst estimate of $4.575 billion, while diluted EPS of $1.82 fell short of a $1.95 estimate. The company reported fourth-quarter adjusted EBITDA of $505.4 million.

Fourth-quarter operating income totaled $322 million for steel operations, $91 million for steel fabrication, and $19 million for metals recycling. Aluminum operations recorded an operating loss of $47 million during the period.

Steel Dynamics said planned maintenance outages reduced fourth-quarter flat-rolled steel production by an estimated 140,000 to 150,000 tons.

The average external steel selling price during the quarter was $1,107 per ton, while the average ferrous scrap cost was $374 per ton.

Full-Year Results and Shipments

For full-year 2025, Steel Dynamics reported net sales of $18.2 billion and net income of $1.2 billion, or $7.99 per diluted share. This compares with net sales of $17.5 billion and net income of $1.5 billion, or $9.84 per diluted share, in 2024.

The operating income declined 24% to $1.5 billion. It reported adjusted EBITDA of $2.2 billion for the year and record annual steel shipments of 13.7 million tons.

Cash flow from operations totaled $273 million in the fourth quarter and $1.4 billion for the full-year 2025. Cash and equivalents stood at $769.9 million as of Dec. 31, 2025, while long-term debt totaled $4.176 billion.

On Nov. 21, 2025, the company issued $800 million in unsecured notes and said the proceeds were used in part to redeem $400 million of 5.000% notes due 2026.

CEO Commentary

CEO Mark D. Millett said, “The aluminum and biocarbon teams continue to make strong progress,” continued Millett. “We have successfully produced finished aluminum flat-rolled products for the industrial and beverage can markets, as well as hot band for the automotive sector. Although there is still work ahead, the team has strong momentum and achieved positive EBITDA in December, positioning us well as we continue commissioning and ramping operations. Additionally, the SDI Biocarbon Solutions team is continuing to refine operations and increase production, providing a significantly lower-carbon supply chain opportunity for our steel and steel fabrication customers.”

Looking ahead, Millett said, “We anticipate that improving market conditions, including increased trade stability and a more favorable interest rate environment, will support solid domestic demand for steel and aluminum products.”

STLD Price Action: Steel Dynamics shares were trading up 1.26% at $183.60 at the time of publication on Monday. The stock is trading at a new 52-week high, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image by Piotr Swat via Shutterstock

data.title: A Millennial Found Their First Apartment Lease From 2013 And Couldn't Believe How Cheap It Was. The Price Felt Unreal By Today's Standards
data.description:
While cleaning out their parents’ garage, one millennial stumbled across a document that stopped them in their tracks: their first apartment lease from 2013. The price? Just $675 a month for a one-bedroom in Phoenix, with an in-unit washer and dryer.

Rent Was Once Reasonable

“I remember at the time thinking it was kinda expensive and being super careful with my budget,” the original poster wrote in a recent Reddit post. “Now that same complex is going for like $1,850 for the exact same floorplan.”

Don't Miss:

  • The AI Marketing Platform Backed by Insiders from Google, Meta, and Amazon — Invest at $0.85/Share
  • Sam Altman Says AI Will Transform the Economy — This Platform Lets Investors Back Private Tech Early

Back then, they made $42,000 a year and could still afford a car, a night out now and then, and even had some money in savings. Today, they earn nearly double that and “somehow have less money at the end of the month.”

Many millennials flooded the comments with their own rent memories. One remembered paying $375 in 2012 for a one-bedroom outside Pittsburgh, which included all utilities and cable. “It was a total piece of sh*t slumlord run spot… but goddamn that was a DEAL,” they said.

Another commenter recalled renting a two-bedroom apartment for $460 in 2011 and said that if they'd known how things would change, they would've saved every extra dollar they could.

Trending: Americans With a Financial Plan Can 4X Their Wealth — Get Your Personalized Plan from a CFP Pro

Then Vs. Now: A Harsh Comparison

Multiple millennials echoed the same shock: rent has tripled, yet wages haven't kept up. “Yep, my salary doubled but housing cost quadrupled,” one person wrote. Others added that even small, run-down apartments they once shared with roommates now go for over $2,000 a month.

The comments painted a bleak picture. Many shared that despite earning far more today, they’re living with roommates, paying half their income on rent, or unable to afford homes in the towns they grew up in.

“I honestly don't understand how I was paying $675 in rent… and still was putting money in retirement and savings,” one person said. “Right now, I make more money, pay less towards housing, my car is paid off, and I feel like I'm barely scraping by.”

A Market Gone Wild

Some blamed corporate landlords and investors who are charging the “absolute max” they can get away with. Others pointed to wage stagnation, zoning rules, and inflation as major culprits. A few who own rental properties admitted costs like taxes and repairs have gone up too, but said they still try to keep prices reasonable.

“My dad owns rentals,” one person shared. “He said he likes to keep the rent fair in hopes they will see the value in his property and take care of it. The last people there were there for 15 years and they left the apartment immaculate.”

Despite the frustration, a few commenters found smart ways to work around the system. One mentioned buying a mobile home in 2018 for $70,000. Their mortgage? $550 a month. Another said they bought a house for $135,000 in 2012. Today, it's worth over $300,000 and their mortgage is less than half what nearby renters pay.

Platforms like Arrived are also giving regular people a shot at investing in rental properties without having to buy an entire home. Starting at just $100, you can own a share of a property and earn rental income; no landlords, no broken toilets.

Whether renting or buying, the same truth kept coming up in the thread: the early 2010s offered a level of affordability that’s nearly vanished. One millennial said it best: “Take me back to those days.”

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Dow Gains Over 100 Points; Steel Dynamics Posts Downbeat Earnings
data.description:
U.S. stocks traded higher this morning, with the Dow Jones index gaining more than 100 points on Monday.

Following the market opening Monday, the Dow traded up 0.33% to 49,259.65 while the NASDAQ gained 0.09% to 23,521.63. The S&P 500 also rose, gaining, 0.26% to 6,933.84.

Check This Out: How To Earn $500 A Month From Goldman Sachs Stock Ahead Of Q4 Earnings

Leading and Lagging Sectors

Materials shares gained by 0.8% on Monday.

In trading on Monday, health care stocks dipped by 0.2%.

Top Headline

Steel Dynamics Inc (NASDAQ:STLD) reported downbeat earnings for the fourth quarter on Monday.

The company posted quarterly earnings of $1.82 per share which missed the analyst consensus estimate of $1.95 per share. The company reported quarterly sales of $4.414 billion which missed the analyst consensus estimate of $4.575 billion.

Equities Trading UP
           

  • Battalion Oil Corp (NYSE:BATL) shares shot up 379% to $6.13 after the company switched its gas processing partner and boosted production.
  • Shares of Brand Engagement Network Inc (NASDAQ:BNAI) got a boost, surging 196% to $48.79 following a strategic partnership announcement that has reinvigorated investor interest.
  • Gaxos.ai Inc. (NASDAQ:GXAI) shares were also up, gaining 76% to $1.97.

Equities Trading DOWN

  • Mingteng International Corp Inc (NASDAQ:MTEN) shares dropped 36% to $2.16.
  • Shares of CN ENERGY GROUP Inc (NASDAQ:CNEY) were down 35% to $0.52 after the company received Nasdaq Delisting determination for minimum bid price deficiency.
  • 60 Degrees Pharmaceuticals Inc (NASDAQ:SXTP) was down, falling 23% to $3.52 after dipping 10% on Friday.

Commodities

In commodity news, oil traded down 0.6% to $60.72 while gold traded up 1.8% at $5,068.50.

Silver traded up 8.1% to $109.565 on Monday, while copper rose 0.5% to $5.9770.

Euro zone

European shares were mixed today. The eurozone's STOXX 600 fell 0.1%, while Spain's IBEX 35 Index rose 0.4%. London's FTSE 100 rose 0.1%, Germany's DAX fell 0.1% and France's CAC 40 fell 0.2% during the session.

Asia Pacific Markets

Asian markets closed mixed on Monday, with Japan's Nikkei falling 1.79%, Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index gaining 0.06% and China's Shanghai Composite declining 0.09%.

Economics

  • The Chicago Fed National Activity Index climbed to -0.04 in November from a reading of -0.42 in October.
  • U.S. durable-goods orders rose 5.3% month-over-month in November following a revised 2.1% plunge in October and topping market estimates of a 3.7% increase.

Photo via Shutterstock
